content.content:
1	# Click model implementation — work packages
2	
3	Scoped from the pilgrimage assessment (`w-mo0gias9`). Each package is sized for a single commission unless noted otherwise.
4	
5	## Package 1: Stacks book schema + click plugin (foundation)
6	
7	**What:** Define the `clicks` and `click_links` book schemas in Stacks. Build a click plugin that owns the book, handles CRUD, enforces immutability (goal frozen after create, conclusion write-once), and validates status transitions (live→parked→live, live/parked→concluded/dropped). Plugin registers with Stacks at guild init.
8	
9	**Depends on:** Nothing. This is the foundation.
10	
11	**Delivers:**
12	- `clicks` book in Stacks (schema as specified in assessment)
13	- `click_links` book in Stacks
14	- Click plugin with create, update-status, conclude, drop, reparent, link operations
15	- Status transition enforcement (live | parked | concluded | dropped)
16	- Immutability enforcement (goal cannot change after creation; conclusion is write-once)
17	- CDC events emitted for all mutations (Laboratory can observe)
18	
19	**Does NOT deliver:** CLI, Oculus view, migration. This is the data layer only.
20	
21	**Estimated complexity:** Medium. Schema is simple; the plugin pattern is established (Clerk is the reference). Main design work is the Stacks book registration API (if not already generalized) and the immutability constraints.
22	
23	## Package 2: Click CLI commands
24	
25	**What:** `nsg click` subcommand tree. All commands listed in the assessment CLI surface sketch.
26	
27	**Depends on:** Package 1 (plugin must exist to call into).
28	
29	**Delivers:**
30	- `nsg click create --goal "..." [--parent <id>]`
31	- `nsg click show --id <id>`
32	- `nsg click list [--status ...] [--root <id>] [--limit N]`
33	- `nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status ...] [--depth N]` — tree renderer with status indicators
34	- `nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — subtree-as-document
35	- `nsg click park/resume/conclude/drop --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]`
36	- `nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type <type>`
37	- `nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <id> [--orphan]`
38	- `nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."` — sugar that concludes + posts commission + creates link
39	- Short ID prefix matching on all `--id` parameters
40	
41	**Does NOT deliver:** Oculus view, migration.
42	
43	**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. Many commands, but each is thin (validate args, call plugin, format output). The `tree` and `extract` renderers are the most involved pieces. Short ID resolution needs a prefix-match query helper.
44	
45	## Package 3: Oculus click view
46	
47	**What:** Purpose-built click visualization in Oculus. NOT a table — a tree or graph view.
48	
49	**Depends on:** Package 1 (needs data to display).
50	
51	**Delivers:**
52	- Click tree view with expandable nesting (full depth, not one-level)
53	- Status indicators per node (live/parked/concluded/dropped with distinct visual treatment)
54	- Goal visible at each node without drilling in
55	- Conclusion visible on hover/click/pane for concluded/dropped nodes
56	- Cross-substrate links visible (click → writ references)
57	- Copyable click IDs
58	- Filter by status, root node
59	
60	**Stretch / future:**
61	- Graph visualization (nodes + edges) — may be a follow-up commission
62	- Drag-and-drop reparenting
63	- Notecard-style spatial arrangement
64	
65	**Does NOT deliver:** Migration, CLI.
66	
67	**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. The tree renderer is the core; Oculus already has component patterns to follow. The graph visualization (Sean's dream view) is probably a separate follow-up unless it's cheap to prototype.
68	
69	## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks
70	
71	**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data into the click book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.
72	
73	**Depends on:** Package 1 (target schema must exist), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification but not strictly required).
74	
75	**Delivers:**
76	- Migration script that reads all `quest` type writs from Clerk books
77	- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships
78	- For concluded/dropped quests: extracts conclusion from body (resolution field or final status section)
79	- Preserves created_at timestamps
80	- Cross-references: creates `click_links` entries for any writ references found in quest bodies (best-effort extraction)
81	- Verification report: before/after counts, orphan check, tree integrity check
82	- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration
83	
84	**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).
85	
86	**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.
87	
88	## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)
89	
90	**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.
91	
92	**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (plugin + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).
93	
94	**Delivers:**
95	- New `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md` replacing the quest skill
96	- Updated Coco agent file: startup uses `nsg click tree` instead of `nsg writ list --type quest`
97	- Session checkpoint discipline: at natural conversation seams, create clicks for decisions reached
98	- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
99	- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`
100	
101	**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.
102	
103	**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.
104	
105	---
106	
107	## Recommended sequencing
108	
109	```
110	Package 1 (schema + plugin)     ← commission first, foundation
111	    │
112	    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
113	    │       │
114	    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
115	    │
116	    ├── Package 3 (Oculus view) ← can parallel with P2
117	    │
118	    └── Package 4 (migration)   ← after P1, before P5
119	```
120	
121	**Critical path:** P1 → P2 → P5. Once P2 lands, Coco can start using clicks for new work even before migration (P4) or Oculus (P3).
122	
123	**Parallelizable:** P3 (Oculus) can run in parallel with P2+P4 once P1 is done.
124	
125	**Total estimated commissions:** 5 (possibly 6 if the Oculus graph view splits from the Oculus tree view).
126

## Package 1: Stacks book schema + click plugin (foundation)

**What:** Define the `clicks` and `click_links` book schemas in Stacks. Build a click plugin that owns the book, handles CRUD, enforces immutability (goal frozen after create, conclusion write-once), and validates status transitions (live→parked→live, live/parked→concluded/dropped). **Depends on:** Nothing. This is the foundation.

**Delivers:**

## Package 2: Click CLI commands

- `nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status ...] [--depth N]` — tree renderer with status indicators
- `nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — subtree-as-document
- `nsg click park/resume/conclude/drop --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]`
- `nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type <type>`
- `nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <id> [--orphan]`
- `nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."` — sugar that concludes + posts commission + creates link
- Short ID prefix matching on all `--id` parameters

**Does NOT deliver:** Oculus view, migration.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. Many commands, but each is thin (validate args, call plugin, format output). The `tree` and `extract` renderers are the most involved pieces. Short ID resolution needs a prefix-match query helper.

## Package 3: Oculus click view

**What:** Purpose-built click visualization in Oculus. NOT a table — a tree or graph view.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (needs data to display).

**Delivers:**
- Click tree view with expandable nesting (full depth, not one-level)
- Status indicators per node (live/parked/concluded/dropped with distinct visual treatment)
- Goal visible at each node without drilling in
- Conclusion visible on hover/click/pane for concluded/dropped nodes
- Cross-substrate links visible (click → writ references)
- Copyable click IDs
- Filter by status, root node

**Stretch / future:**
- Graph visualization (nodes + edges) — may be a follow-up commission
- Drag-and-drop reparenting
- Notecard-style spatial arrangement

**Does NOT deliver:** Migration, CLI.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. The tree renderer is the core; Oculus already has component patterns to follow. The graph visualization (Sean's dream view) is probably a separate follow-up unless it's cheap to prototype.

## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks

**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data into the click book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (target schema must exist), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification but not strictly required).

**Delivers:**
- Migration script that reads all `quest` type writs from Clerk books
- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships
- For concluded/dropped quests: extracts conclusion from body (resolution field or final status section)
- Preserves created_at timestamps
- Cross-references: creates `click_links` entries for any writ references found in quest bodies (best-effort extraction)
- Verification report: before/after counts, orphan check, tree integrity check
- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration

**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.

## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)

**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.

**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (plugin + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).

**Delivers:**
- New `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md` replacing the quest skill
- Updated Coco agent file: startup uses `nsg click tree` instead of `nsg writ list --type quest`
- Session checkpoint discipline: at natural conversation seams, create clicks for decisions reached
- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`

**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.

**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.

---

## Recommended sequencing

```
Package 1 (schema + plugin)     ← commission first, foundation
    │
    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
    │       │
    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
    │
    ├── Package 3 (Oculus view) ← can parallel with P2
    │
    └── Package 4 (migration)   ← after P1, before P5
```

**Critical path:** P1 → P2 → P5. Once P2 lands, Coco can start using clicks for new work even before migration (P4) or Oculus (P3).

**Parallelizable:** P3 (Oculus) can run in parallel with P2+P4 once P1 is done.

**Total estimated commissions:** 5 (possibly 6 if the Oculus graph view splits from the Oculus tree view).

Registers its books with Stacks at guild init. Follows the Clerk plugin as architectural reference.

- `clicks` book in Stacks (schema as specified in assessment)
- `click_links` book in Stacks
- Ratchet API: create, update-status, conclude, drop, reparent, link operations
- Status transition enforcement (live | parked | concluded | dropped)
- Immutability enforcement (goal cannot change after creation; conclusion is write-once)
- CDC events emitted through Stacks for all mutations (Laboratory can observe)

**Does NOT deliver:** CLI, Oculus view, migration. This is the apparatus + data layer only.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. Schema is simple; the plugin pattern is established (Clerk is the reference). Main design work is ensuring the Stacks book registration API generalizes cleanly for a second plugin (Clerk is currently the primary consumer), and the immutability constraints.

# Click model implementation — work packages

## Package 1: Stacks book schema + click plugin (foundation)

**What:** Define the `clicks` and `click_links` book schemas in Stacks. Build a click plugin that owns the book, handles CRUD, enforces immutability (goal frozen after create, conclusion write-once), and validates status transitions (live→parked→live, live/parked→concluded/dropped). Plugin registers with Stacks at guild init.

**Depends on:** Nothing. This is the foundation.

**Delivers:**
- `clicks` book in Stacks (schema as specified in assessment)
- `click_links` book in Stacks
- Click plugin with create, update-status, conclude, drop, reparent, link operations
- Status transition enforcement (live | parked | concluded | dropped)
- Immutability enforcement (goal cannot change after creation; conclusion is write-once)
- CDC events emitted for all mutations (Laboratory can observe)

**Does NOT deliver:** CLI, Oculus view, migration. This is the data layer only.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. Schema is simple; the plugin pattern is established (Clerk is the reference). Main design work is the Stacks book registration API (if not already generalized) and the immutability constraints.

## Package 2: Click CLI commands

**What:** `nsg click` subcommand tree. All commands listed in the assessment CLI surface sketch.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (plugin must exist to call into).

**Delivers:**
- `nsg click create --goal "..." [--parent <id>]`
- `nsg click show --id <id>`
- `nsg click list [--status ...] [--root <id>] [--limit N]`
- `nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status ...] [--depth N]` — tree renderer with status indicators
- `nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — subtree-as-document
- `nsg click park/resume/conclude/drop --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]`
- `nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type <type>`
- `nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <id> [--orphan]`
- `nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."` — sugar that concludes + posts commission + creates link
- Short ID prefix matching on all `--id` parameters

**Does NOT deliver:** Oculus view, migration.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. Many commands, but each is thin (validate args, call plugin, format output). The `tree` and `extract` renderers are the most involved pieces. Short ID resolution needs a prefix-match query helper.

- Copyable click IDs
- Filter by status, root node

**Stretch / future:**
- Graph visualization (nodes + edges) — may be a follow-up commission
- Drag-and-drop reparenting
- Notecard-style spatial arrangement

**Does NOT deliver:** Migration, CLI.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. The tree renderer is the core; Oculus already has component patterns to follow. The graph visualization (Sean's dream view) is probably a separate follow-up unless it's cheap to prototype.

## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks

**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data into the click book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (target schema must exist), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification but not strictly required).

**Delivers:**

- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships

- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration

**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.

## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)

**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.

**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (plugin + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).

**Delivers:**

- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`

**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.

**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.

---

## Recommended sequencing

```
Package 1 (schema + plugin)     ← commission first, foundation
    │
    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
    │       │
    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
    │

## Package 1: Ratchet apparatus (foundation)

**What:** Build the Ratchet plugin. It owns the `clicks` and `click_links` books in Stacks, handles CRUD, enforces immutability (goal frozen after create, conclusion write-once), and validates status transitions (live→parked→live, live/parked→concluded/dropped). Registers its books with Stacks at guild init. Follows the Clerk plugin as architectural reference.

**Depends on:** Nothing. This is the foundation.

**Delivers:**
- Ratchet plugin (`packages/plugins/ratchet/` or similar)
- `clicks` book in Stacks (schema as specified in assessment)
- `click_links` book in Stacks
- Ratchet API: create, update-status, conclude, drop, reparent, link operations
- Status transition enforcement (live | parked | concluded | dropped)
- Immutability enforcement (goal cannot change after creation; conclusion is write-once)
- CDC events emitted through Stacks for all mutations (Laboratory can observe)
- Guild configuration: Ratchet registered as a plugin in `guild.json`

**Does NOT deliver:** CLI, Oculus view, migration. This is the apparatus + data layer only.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. Schema is simple; the plugin pattern is established (Clerk is the reference). Main design work is ensuring the Stacks book registration API generalizes cleanly for a second plugin (Clerk is currently the primary consumer), and the immutability constraints.

## Package 2: Click CLI commands

**What:** `nsg click` subcommand tree contributed by the Ratchet plugin. All commands listed in the assessment CLI surface sketch.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus must exist to call into).

**Delivers:**
- `nsg click create --goal "..." [--parent <id>]`
- `nsg click show --id <id>`
- `nsg click list [--status ...] [--root <id>] [--limit N]`
- `nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status ...] [--depth N]` — tree renderer with status indicators
- `nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — subtree-as-document
- `nsg click park/resume/conclude/drop --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]`
- `nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type <type>`
- `nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <id> [--orphan]`
- `nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."` — sugar that concludes + posts commission + creates link
- Short ID prefix matching on all `--id` parameters

**Does NOT deliver:** Oculus view, migration.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. Many commands, but each is thin (validate args, call plugin, format output). The `tree` and `extract` renderers are the most involved pieces. Short ID resolution needs a prefix-match query helper.

## Package 3: Oculus click view

**What:** Purpose-built click visualization in Oculus. NOT a table — a tree or graph view.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (needs data to display).

**Delivers:**
- Click tree view with expandable nesting (full depth, not one-level)
- Status indicators per node (live/parked/concluded/dropped with distinct visual treatment)
- Goal visible at each node without drilling in
- Conclusion visible on hover/click/pane for concluded/dropped nodes
- Cross-substrate links visible (click → writ references)
- Copyable click IDs
- Filter by status, root node

**Stretch / future:**
- Graph visualization (nodes + edges) — may be a follow-up commission
- Drag-and-drop reparenting
- Notecard-style spatial arrangement

**Does NOT deliver:** Migration, CLI.

**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. The tree renderer is the core; Oculus already has component patterns to follow. The graph visualization (Sean's dream view) is probably a separate follow-up unless it's cheap to prototype.

## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks

**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data into the click book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (target schema must exist), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification but not strictly required).

**Delivers:**
- Migration script that reads all `quest` type writs from Clerk books
- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships
- For concluded/dropped quests: extracts conclusion from body (resolution field or final status section)
- Preserves created_at timestamps
- Cross-references: creates `click_links` entries for any writ references found in quest bodies (best-effort extraction)
- Verification report: before/after counts, orphan check, tree integrity check
- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration

**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.

## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)

**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.

**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (plugin + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).

**Delivers:**
- New `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md` replacing the quest skill
- Updated Coco agent file: startup uses `nsg click tree` instead of `nsg writ list --type quest`
- Session checkpoint discipline: at natural conversation seams, create clicks for decisions reached
- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`

**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.

**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.

---

## Recommended sequencing

```
Package 1 (schema + plugin)     ← commission first, foundation
    │
    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
    │       │
    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
    │
    ├── Package 3 (Oculus view) ← can parallel with P2
    │
    └── Package 4 (migration)   ← after P1, before P5
```

**Critical path:** P1 → P2 → P5. Once P2 lands, Coco can start using clicks for new work even before migration (P4) or Oculus (P3).

**Parallelizable:** P3 (Oculus) can run in parallel with P2+P4 once P1 is done.

**Total estimated commissions:** 5 (possibly 6 if the Oculus graph view splits from the Oculus tree view).

## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks

**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data from Clerk's writs book into Ratchet's clicks book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.

**Depends on:** Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus must exist as target), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification via CLI but not strictly required).

**Delivers:**
- Migration script that reads all `quest` type writs from Clerk books
- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships
- For concluded/dropped quests: extracts conclusion from body (resolution field or final status section)
- Preserves created_at timestamps
- Cross-references: creates `click_links` entries for any writ references found in quest bodies (best-effort extraction)
- Verification report: before/after counts, orphan check, tree integrity check
- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration

**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).

**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.

## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)

**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.

**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (Ratchet apparatus + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).

**Delivers:**
- New `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md` replacing the quest skill
- Updated Coco agent file: startup uses `nsg click tree` instead of `nsg writ list --type quest`
- Session checkpoint discipline: at natural conversation seams, create clicks for decisions reached
- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`

**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.

**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.

---

## Recommended sequencing

```
Package 1 (schema + plugin)     ← commission first, foundation
    │
    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
    │       │
    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
    │
    ├── Package 3 (Oculus view) ← can parallel with P2
    │
    └── Package 4 (migration)   ← after P1, before P5
```

**Critical path:** P1 → P2 → P5. Once P2 lands, Coco can start using clicks for new work even before migration (P4) or Oculus (P3).

**Parallelizable:** P3 (Oculus) can run in parallel with P2+P4 once P1 is done.

**Total estimated commissions:** 5 (possibly 6 if the Oculus graph view splits from the Oculus tree view).

190	## Emerging themes from interview
191	
192	1. **The tree is the product, not the prose.** Sean's primary value from quests is the hierarchical map of the problem space — umbrellas, groupings, parent-child relationships. The body content is secondary at best, noise at worst. This inverts the original design assumption (bodies are the primary artifact, tree is the organizational mechanism).
193	
194	2. **"Coco as sole interface" works conceptually but fails in practice.** Coco doesn't load enough context; Sean ends up doing the remembering. The intermediation that was supposed to shield Sean from substrate complexity instead creates an opaque layer where Sean can't verify Coco's interpretations.
195

Each is individually patchable; collectively they suggest the substrate is pulling toward its designed purpose.

199	
200	5. **The promotion boundary is undesigned.** Quest→commission is manual copy-paste through Coco with no linking back. The system has no concept of "this inquiry produced this work."

file.content:
## Emerging themes from interview

1. **The tree is the product, not the prose.** Sean's primary value from quests is the hierarchical map of the problem space — umbrellas, groupings, parent-child relationships. The body content is secondary at best, noise at worst. This inverts the original design assumption (bodies are the primary artifact, tree is the organizational mechanism).

2. **"Coco as sole interface" works conceptually but fails in practice.** Coco doesn't load enough context; Sean ends up doing the remembering. The intermediation that was supposed to shield Sean from substrate complexity instead creates an opaque layer where Sean can't verify Coco's interpretations.

3. **The obligation model leaks everywhere.** Status model, Oculus views, default filters, status pills, nesting depth — the writ substrate's obligation assumptions show up in every surface quests touch. Each is individually patchable; collectively they suggest the substrate is pulling toward its designed purpose.

4. **Quests want to be small, linked, and structured; they're currently big, isolated, and narrative.** The ideal shape (one-line goal, status sentence, linked items, brief decision rationale) is almost the opposite of the current template (six-section synthesis document).

5. **The promotion boundary is undesigned.** Quest→commission is manual copy-paste through Coco with no linking back. The system has no concept of "this inquiry produced this work."

## Design direction emerging from interview + analysis (2026-04-16)

### The "click" model

**Name:** click — the sound of a decision snapping into place. A ratchet clicking forward.

**Core concept:** Replace heavy synthesis-document quests with atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree. The tree is the product; prose is minimal. Exploration lives in transcripts; the click tree captures the *decisions* that exploration produced.

**Data model:**

```
clicks (new Stacks book)
├── id                    (string, generated)
├── parent_id             (string, nullable — null = root)
├── goal                  (string, immutable after creation)
├── status                (enum: live | parked | concluded | cancelled)
├── conclusion            (string, nullable — write-once at resolution)
├── created_session_id    (string — Claude session ID)
├── resolved_session_id   (string, nullable — session ID at conclusion)
├── created_at            (timestamp)
├── resolved_at           (timestamp, nullable)

click_links
├── source_id             (string, FK to clicks)
├── target_id             (string — can be click ID or writ ID, cross-substrate)
├── link_type             (enum: related | commissioned | supersedes | depends-on)
├── created_at            (timestamp)
```

**Key properties:**
- Immutable on create: goal never changes. Cancel and reframe if wrong.
- Append-only conclusion: write-once at resolution. 1-3 paragraphs of rationale.
- No body editing, no file-canonical dance, no closure ritual.
- Four statuses: live | parked | concluded | cancelled.
- Cross-substrate links: clicks can link to writs (mandates, briefs) via `commissioned` link type.
- Children are the todo list: open questions become child clicks, not prose sections.

### The click model — Ratchet apparatus

**Ratchet** — the apparatus (new framework plugin, peer to Clerk/Spider/Astrolabe). The mechanism that advances forward. Owns the clicks book in Stacks, enforces immutability and status transitions, exposes the click API.

**Click** — the record type. The sound of a decision snapping into place. Each click is one irreversible advancement of the ratchet.

**Core concept:** Replace heavy synthesis-document quests with atomic, immutable decision-nodes in a tree. The tree is the product; prose is minimal. Exploration lives in transcripts; the click tree captures the *decisions* that exploration produced.

**Architecture:** Ratchet is a separate plugin from Clerk. Clerk manages obligations (writs); Ratchet manages inquiry (clicks). They share the Stacks storage layer but are separate concerns. Cross-substrate links (click → writ) are typed references.

**Data model:**

```
clicks (Ratchet's Stacks book)
├── id                    (string, generated)
├── parent_id             (string, nullable — null = root)
├── goal                  (string, immutable after creation)
├── status                (enum: live | parked | concluded | dropped)
├── conclusion            (string, nullable — write-once at resolution)
├── created_session_id    (string — Claude session ID)
├── resolved_session_id   (string, nullable — session ID at conclusion/drop)
├── created_at            (timestamp)
├── resolved_at           (timestamp, nullable)

click_links
├── source_id             (string, FK to clicks)
├── target_id             (string — can be click ID or writ ID, cross-substrate)
├── link_type             (enum: related | commissioned | supersedes | depends-on)
├── created_at            (timestamp)
```

**Key properties:**
- Immutable on create: goal never changes. Drop and reframe if wrong.
- Append-only conclusion: write-once at resolution. 1-3 paragraphs of rationale.
- No body editing, no file-canonical dance, no closure ritual.
- Four statuses: live | parked | concluded | dropped.
- Cross-substrate links: clicks can link to writs (mandates, briefs) via `commissioned` link type.
- Children are the todo list: open questions become child clicks, not prose sections.
- Boundary: clicks are always questions/inquiries, never tasks. Tasks are mandates (writs).

- Four statuses: live | parked | concluded | dropped.
- Cross-substrate links: clicks can link to writs (mandates, briefs) via `commissioned` link type.
- Children are the todo list: open questions become child clicks, not prose sections.

**Boundary principle:** clicks are always questions/inquiries, never tasks. Tasks are mandates (writs). A click's conclusion may *imply* work, which becomes a commission linked back via `commissioned`. The click tree is the reasoning record; the writ tree is the work record.

**CLI surface:**

```bash
# Create
nsg click create --goal "Should we migrate to a Stacks book?" [--parent <id>]

# Navigate
nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status live] [--depth 3]
nsg click show --id <id>
nsg click list [--status live] [--root <id>] [--limit 100]
nsg click extract --id <id> [--full]    # subtree as structured document

# Lifecycle
nsg click park --id <id>
nsg click resume --id <id>
nsg click conclude --id <id> --conclusion "..."
nsg click cancel --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]

# Links and structure
nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type related
nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."
nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <new-parent-id>
```

**Short IDs everywhere:** CLI accepts prefix matches (e.g., `w-mo0gias9`), errors on ambiguity.

**`extract` is the continuity mechanism:** renders a subtree as a structured markdown document on demand. Always current by construction. Replaces file-canonical quest bodies as the way Coco loads context.

**Catalog items dissolved by the click model:** A3, A4, B1-B4, C1-C3, C8, D1-D2, E1-E5, E6, F1, H2, H3, J4, J5 — roughly 20 of 38 items eliminated by the shape change alone.

### What the click model does NOT solve (still needs work)

- **C5, C6, C9** — Oculus needs a purpose-built click view (graph, not table). Separate commission.

All three files updated with Ratchet as the apparatus name and `dropped` as the terminal status. Here's what we've got:

| File | Status |
|---|---|
| `.scratch/click-model-assessment.md` | ✅ Updated — Ratchet architecture throughout |
| `.scratch/click-model-work-packages.md` | ✅ Updated — P1 is "Ratchet apparatus," dependencies corrected |
| `.scratch/quest-substrate-friction-catalog.md` | ✅ Updated — design direction section reflects Ratchet + dropped status |

Ready for your review. Want to look them over before we publish the assessment to its permanent location and start thinking about dispatching P1?

message.content: so whats the part i can poost as a commission? just copy P1? where does context for the planner/implementer come from? do we need an arch doc in nexus/docs/architecture/apparatus, or somewhere else?

content.text:
Good question — and it exposes a real gap. P1 as written is scoped for *us* (we know the full conversation history), but an autonomous agent getting this as a commission would be missing most of the context. They'd need:

1. **What a click is and why it exists** — the design rationale
2. **The data model** — schema, invariants, status transitions
3. **Where it fits architecturally** — peer to Clerk, owns its own Stacks books
4. **A reference implementation to follow** — Clerk as the pattern

The assessment has all of this, but it's written as a pilgrimage conclusion (evidence, interview findings, recommendation), not as an architecture spec an implementer can build from.

## What I'd do

**Two artifacts:**

1. **Architecture doc** at `/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/ratchet.md` — the permanent design spec. Lives in the framework repo because Ratchet is a framework plugin. Covers: purpose, data model, invariants, status transitions, API surface, relationship to Clerk/Stacks, CLI commands. This is what the planner and implementer read. Concise, structured, buildable-from.

2. **The commission brief** — references the architecture doc. The brief itself is short: "Build the Ratchet apparatus as specified in `docs/architecture/ratchet.md`. Follow Clerk (`packages/plugins/clerk/`) as the implementation pattern. Package 1 scope: plugin, Stacks books, API, status enforcement, CDC events. Does not include CLI, Oculus, or migration."

The architecture doc does the heavy lifting. The commission just points at it and scopes the work.

The assessment (pilgrimage conclusion) goes to `docs/archive/design-sessions/` as the *reasoning record* — how we got here, not what to build. The implementer probably never reads it, but it's there if someone asks "why does Ratchet exist?"

## Proposed file locations

```
/workspace/nexus/docs/architecture/ratchet.md     ← design spec (new)
/workspace/nexus-mk2/docs/archive/design-sessions/click-model-assessment.md  ← pilgrimage conclusion (publish from .scratch)
```

Want me to draft the architecture doc? I can extract the buildable parts from the assessment and friction catalog, strip the evidence/interview material, and produce something an implementer can work from directly.

content: Designing "Ratchet," a new framework plugin that replaces quests with small immutable decision-nodes called "clicks." We finished the design spec and work packages. Next action: draft the architecture doc at nexus/docs/architecture/ratchet.md so we can post P1 as a commission.

1	# Click model implementation — work packages
2	
3	Scoped from the pilgrimage assessment (`w-mo0gias9`). Each package is sized for a single commission unless noted otherwise.
4	
5	**Apparatus:** Ratchet — a new framework plugin, peer to Clerk/Spider/Astrolabe. Owns the clicks book in Stacks. Manages the inquiry/decision domain, analogous to Clerk managing the obligation domain.
6	
7	## Package 1: Ratchet apparatus (foundation)
8	
9	**What:** Build the Ratchet plugin. It owns the `clicks` and `click_links` books in Stacks, handles CRUD, enforces immutability (goal frozen after create, conclusion write-once), and validates status transitions (live→parked→live, live/parked→concluded/dropped). Registers its books with Stacks at guild init. Follows the Clerk plugin as architectural reference.
10	
11	**Depends on:** Nothing. This is the foundation.
12	
13	**Delivers:**
14	- Ratchet plugin (`packages/plugins/ratchet/` or similar)
15	- `clicks` book in Stacks (schema as specified in assessment)
16	- `click_links` book in Stacks
17	- Ratchet API: create, update-status, conclude, drop, reparent, link operations
18	- Status transition enforcement (live | parked | concluded | dropped)
19	- Immutability enforcement (goal cannot change after creation; conclusion is write-once)
20	- CDC events emitted through Stacks for all mutations (Laboratory can observe)
21	
22	**Does NOT deliver:** CLI, Oculus view, migration. This is the apparatus + data layer only.
23	
24	**Estimated complexity:** Medium. Schema is simple; the plugin pattern is established (Clerk is the reference). Main design work is ensuring the Stacks book registration API generalizes cleanly for a second plugin (Clerk is currently the primary consumer), and the immutability constraints.
25	
26	## Package 2: Click CLI commands
27	
28	**What:** `nsg click` subcommand tree contributed by the Ratchet plugin. All commands listed in the assessment CLI surface sketch.
29	
30	**Depends on:** Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus must exist to call into).
31	
32	**Delivers:**
33	- `nsg click create --goal "..." [--parent <id>]`
34	- `nsg click show --id <id>`
35	- `nsg click list [--status ...] [--root <id>] [--limit N]`
36	- `nsg click tree [--root <id>] [--status ...] [--depth N]` — tree renderer with status indicators
37	- `nsg click extract --id <id> [--full] [--format md|json]` — subtree-as-document
38	- `nsg click park/resume/conclude/drop --id <id> [--conclusion "..."]`
39	- `nsg click link --from <id> --to <id> --type <type>`
40	- `nsg click reparent --id <id> --parent <id> [--orphan]`
41	- `nsg click commission --id <id> --conclusion "..." --brief "..."` — sugar that concludes + posts commission + creates link
42	- Short ID prefix matching on all `--id` parameters
43	
44	**Does NOT deliver:** Oculus view, migration.
45	
46	**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. Many commands, but each is thin (validate args, call plugin, format output). The `tree` and `extract` renderers are the most involved pieces. Short ID resolution needs a prefix-match query helper.
47	
48	## Package 3: Oculus click view
49	
50	**What:** Purpose-built click visualization in Oculus. NOT a table — a tree or graph view. Reads from Ratchet's Stacks books.
51	
52	**Depends on:** Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus must exist; needs data to display).
53	
54	**Delivers:**
55	- Click tree view with expandable nesting (full depth, not one-level)
56	- Status indicators per node (live/parked/concluded/dropped with distinct visual treatment)
57	- Goal visible at each node without drilling in
58	- Conclusion visible on hover/click/pane for concluded/dropped nodes
59	- Cross-substrate links visible (click → writ references)
60	- Copyable click IDs
61	- Filter by status, root node
62	
63	**Stretch / future:**
64	- Graph visualization (nodes + edges) — may be a follow-up commission
65	- Drag-and-drop reparenting
66	- Notecard-style spatial arrangement
67	
68	**Does NOT deliver:** Migration, CLI.
69	
70	**Estimated complexity:** Medium-high. The tree renderer is the core; Oculus already has component patterns to follow. The graph visualization (Sean's dream view) is probably a separate follow-up unless it's cheap to prototype.
71	
72	## Package 4: Migration from quest writs to clicks
73	
74	**What:** Migrate existing quest writ data from Clerk's writs book into Ratchet's clicks book. Map quest writs to clicks, preserve tree structure, map statuses, extract goals from bodies.
75	
76	**Depends on:** Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus must exist as target), Package 2 (nice-to-have for verification via CLI but not strictly required).
77	
78	**Delivers:**
79	- Migration script that reads all `quest` type writs from Clerk books
80	- Maps each quest to a click: extracts goal from body (first line or Goal section), maps status (`open→live`, `completed→concluded`, `cancelled→dropped`), preserves parent-child relationships
81	- For concluded/dropped quests: extracts conclusion from body (resolution field or final status section)
82	- Preserves created_at timestamps
83	- Cross-references: creates `click_links` entries for any writ references found in quest bodies (best-effort extraction)
84	- Verification report: before/after counts, orphan check, tree integrity check
85	- Deprecation of `quest` writ type from guild.json after migration
86	
87	**Does NOT deliver:** Removal of quest files from vibers repo (separate cleanup), Oculus view, historical quest body archival (bodies are already in git history via vibers repo).
88	
89	**Estimated complexity:** Medium. The mapping is straightforward for most quests. The tricky part is extracting goals and conclusions from free-form quest bodies — some will need manual review. A "migration review" pass with Coco is probably needed after the script runs.
90	
91	## Package 5: Coco integration (skill + habits)
92	
93	**What:** Update Coco's quest skill, startup ritual, and session habits to use clicks instead of quests.
94	
95	**Depends on:** Packages 1-2 (Ratchet apparatus + CLI must exist). Package 4 (migration should be done so the data is there).
96	
97	**Delivers:**
98	- New `.claude/skills/clicks/SKILL.md` replacing the quest skill
99	- Updated Coco agent file: startup uses `nsg click tree` instead of `nsg writ list --type quest`
100	- Session checkpoint discipline: at natural conversation seams, create clicks for decisions reached
101	- Wrap-up ritual: simplified (no bodies to update, no files to snapshot)
102	- Retirement of `.claude/skills/quests/SKILL.md`
103	
104	**Does NOT deliver:** This is a documentation/configuration change, not code.
105	
106	**Estimated complexity:** Low. Mostly writing skill docs and updating agent instructions.
107	
108	---
109	
110	## Recommended sequencing
111	
112	```
113	Package 1 (Ratchet apparatus)   ← commission first, foundation
114	    │
115	    ├── Package 2 (CLI)         ← commission after P1 lands
116	    │       │
117	    │       └── Package 5 (Coco integration) ← after P2, needs working CLI
118	    │
119	    ├── Package 3 (Oculus view) ← can parallel with P2
120	    │
121	    └── Package 4 (migration)   ← after P1, before P5
122	```
123	
124	**Critical path:** P1 → P2 → P5. Once P2 lands, Coco can start using clicks for new work even before migration (P4) or Oculus (P3).
125	
126	**Parallelizable:** P3 (Oculus) can run in parallel with P2+P4 once P1 is done.
127	
128	**Total estimated commissions:** 5 (possibly 6 if the Oculus graph view splits from the Oculus tree view).

file.content:
# The Clerk — API Contract

Status: **Draft**

Package: `@shardworks/clerk-apparatus` · Plugin id: `clerk`

---

## Purpose

The Clerk is the guild's obligation authority. It receives commissions from the patron, issues writs that formally record what is owed, manages the lifecycle of those writs through to completion or failure, and maintains the Ledger — the guild's book of work.

The Clerk owns the boundary between "what is asked for" and "how it gets done." A commission arrives; the Clerk creates a mandate writ. When work completes, the Clerk records the outcome. Connecting writs to sessions is the job of the rigging system — the Spider assembles rigs, and engines execute the work. The Clerk tracks the obligation, not the execution.

The Clerk does **not** execute work. It does not launch sessions, manage rigs, or orchestrate engines. It tracks obligations: what has been commissioned, what state each obligation is in, and whether the guild has fulfilled its commitments. When the Clockworks and rigging system exist, the Clerk will integrate with them via lifecycle events and signals.

Writs can be organized into parent/child hierarchies for decomposing complex work. A parent writ stays in `open` status while its children are being processed. Failure cascades upward (child failure fails the parent) and cancellation cascades downward (parent termination cancels non-terminal children).

---

## Dependencies

```
requires: ['stacks']
recommends: ['oculus']
```

- **The Stacks** (required) — persists writs in the `writs` book and links in the `links` book. All writ state lives here.
- **Oculus** (recommended) — provides observability dashboard.

---

## Kit Interface

The Clerk consumes `writTypes` kit contributions. Kits may declare writ types that are merged into the guild's type vocabulary at startup.

```typescript
consumes: ['writTypes']
```

---

## Support Kit

```typescript
supportKit: {
  books: {
    writs: {
      indexes: [
        'status', 'type', 'createdAt', 'parentId',
        ['status', 'type'], ['status', 'createdAt'], ['parentId', 'status'],
      ],
    },
    links: {
      indexes: ['sourceId', 'targetId', 'type', ['sourceId', 'type'], ['targetId', 'type']],
    },
  },
  tools: [
    commissionPost,
    writShow,
    writList,
    writComplete,
    writFail,
    writCancel,
    writPublish,
    writLink,
    writUnlink,
    writTypesTool,
  ],
},
```

### `commission-post` tool

Post a new commission. Creates a writ in `open` status by default, or in `new` (draft) status when `draft: true` is passed. Supports creating child writs via `parentId`.

| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|-----------|------|----------|-------------|
| `title` | `string` | yes | Short description of the work |
| `body` | `string` | yes | Full spec — what to do, acceptance criteria, context |
